title: Time-Embedded Algorithm Unrolling for Computational MRI
abstract: Algorithm unrolling methods have proven powerful for solving the regularized least squares problem in computational magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). These approaches unfold an iterative algorithm with a fixed number of iterations, typically alternating between a neural network-based proximal operator for regularization, a data fidelity operation and auxiliary updates with learnable parameters. While the connection to optimization methods dictate that the proximal operator network should be shared across unrolls, this can introduce artifacts or blurring. Heuristically, practitioners have shown that using distinct networks may be beneficial, but this significantly increases the number of learnable parameters, making it challenging to prevent overfitting. To address these shortcomings, by taking inspirations from proximal operators with varying thresholds in approximate message passing (AMP) and the success of time-embedding in diffusion models, we propose a time-embedded algorithm unrolling scheme for inverse problems. Specifically, we introduce a novel perspective on the iteration-dependent proximal operation in vector AMP (VAMP) and the subsequent Onsager correction in the context of algorithm unrolling, framing them as a time-embedded neural network. Similarly, the scalar weights in the data fidelity operation and its associated Onsager correction are cast as time-dependent learnable parameters. Our extensive experiments on the fastMRI dataset, spanning various acceleration rates and datasets, demonstrate that our method effectively reduces aliasing artifacts and mitigates noise amplification, achieving state-of-the-art performance. Furthermore, we show that our time-embedding strategy extends to existing algorithm unrolling approaches, enhancing reconstruction quality without increasing the computational complexity significantly. Code available at https://github.com/JN-Yun/TE-Unrolling-MRI.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes to incorporate time information into the proximal operator within the algorithm unrolling scheme for accelerated MRI reconstruction, in order to address the trade-off in unrolled networks where sharing a proximal operator across iterations can cause artifacts, while using distinct networks per iteration leads to overfitting. The proposed method leads to on-par results with non-weight-sharing unrolling methods weight-sharing unrolling methods on uniformly 4x,6x,8x undersampled MRI data from fastMRI.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
- The idea of the time-dependent proximal operator to avoid using non-weight-sharing regularizers has some merits.
- The experiments are comprehensive

Weaknesses:
- The gains brought the proposed method seem marginal both quantitatively and qualitatively (Tab 1, Fig 3). Even the performance between the weight-sharing regularizers and the non-weight-sharing regularizers is close already (Tab 1). 
-  The core technical contribution of the paper is the time-embedding applied to the proximal operator within a VAMP-inspired framework, which seems limited: AMP has been explored in algorithm unrolling as also noted by the authors, and the time is embeded using FiLM.

QUESTIONS:
The gains of the proposed methods over weight-sharing methods are not obvious either.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper introduces a Time-Embedded Algorithm Unrolling framework for MRI inverse problems, integrating diffusion-model time embeddings with AMP/VAMP’s Onsager corrections and data-fidelity weights as learnable, iteration-dependent functions. Each unrolled iteration leverages step-specific FiLM networks and residual corrections to achieve high-fidelity reconstructions.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
# Strengths:

- Extending FiLM-based networks to learn iteration-dependent residual corrections is novel.
- Recasting unrolling methods through an information-theoretic and free-energy perspective.
- The framework seems generalizes to any iterative reconstruction algorithm. The experimental results are impressive!

# Weaknesses:
- Section 3 maps the diffusion-model Markov chain to a VAMP-inspired unrolling, assuming unbiased estimators and free-energy conservation in Onsager corrections, but omits a formal proof of second-moment matching or energy invariance.
- Lacks formal convergence/error bounds. Stability of the deep FiLM layers with time embeddings is only empirically demonstrated; no bounds on the Lipschitz constant or gradient explosion/vanishing are provided for large T.

QUESTIONS:
- It would be better to include ablation studies on embedding dimension, sinusoidal frequencies, FiLM layer width, with learning curves in the appendix.
- Validate on real multi-coil, non-Gaussian noise data or simulate Rician/Poisson noise and non-uniform sampling masks.
- Please report mean ± std for PSNR/SSIM.. and perform significance tests (e.g. t-test) to confirm improvements.

LIMITATIONS:
The authors have not discussed potential reconstruction artefacts arising from noise-model mismatch (e.g. Rician or Poisson) or motion-induced errors. I'd like to recommend adding a discussion of these limitations and their possible diagnostic implications.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
In this paper, the authors proposed a time-dependent unrolling-based neural network for fast MRI reconstruction. Simulation experiment demonstrates its effectiveness. Addressing the typical inverse problem of compressed sensing MRI, this paper could trigger the interest of a wide range of audiences in computational imaging.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
(1) This paper borrows the advantages from the time-embedding design in diffusion model and vector AMP algorithm and successfully apply them in CS-MRI.
(2) This paper is well organized and easy to read.
(3) Experiments on NYU fastMRI dataset are adequate and evaluation is performed on different un-rolling baselines.

Weaknesses:
(1) Key motivation is vague.

QUESTIONS:
(1) "Time" in the "time-embedded" is referred to as the diffusion and denoising step in diffusion models, whereas it is the iteration step in unrolling-based methods in CS-MRI. The authors might want to clarify this point.
(2) Following the last comment, it would be interesting to discuss the time-embedded networks in dynamic MRI.
(3) In Figure 1, E is not undefined in this figure.
(4) Authors mentioned the data-scarce condition in CS-MRI, which limits the unshared networks. Please detail such limitations.
(5) In Table 1, what does the spades denote?
(6) Please explain if the network in your method is shared except for the time condition.
(7) Could you explain more about why TE works better on VSQP in Section 4.4?
(8) In Equation 16, what is the value of \tau?

LIMITATIONS:
(1) There are many hyperparameters in the proposed method, and I am curious if it could be well generalized to other datasets.
(2) I would encourage the code to be publicized.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The authors propose Time-Embedded Algorithm Unrolling (TE-Unroll) for DL-based inverse problems in MRI.

Building on the link between vector AMP and diffusion models, they: (i) make the proximal network explicitly time‐conditioned via FiLM-style embeddings; (ii) treat the data-fidelity weight and Onsager term as learnable, time-varying scalars; and (iii) show how the idea plugs into multiple unrolling schemes (VSQP, ADMM) with both ResNet and U-Net proximals. 

Experiments on the fastMRI knee (PD / PD-FS) and brain (T2) data at R = 4/6/8 undersampling demonstrate consistent PSNR/SSIM gains over shared and unshared baselines while adding <15 % extra parameters (e.g., 1.96 M vs 1.72 M for U-Net) . Extensive ablations (unroll depth, alternative optimisers) and qualitative slices support the claims. 

The paper is very interesting, in terms of embedding the # of step into the unrolled network, I do have some concerns regarding the evaluations, comparisons. I will elaborate later.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
For this paper i would like to put strengths and weaknesses together as follows:

1. This paper is very well-written, clear, one small thing is that the figures are not in PDF format, probably png, which makes it look blurry when i zoomed in to see the results, a small thing.

2. I agree embedding time information into unrolled framework is important and could benefit the efficiency with non-shared regularizer. However, its still unclear to me, in which situation, shared regularizer performs worse compared to non-shared regularizer. From my experience, it's not always true, depends on the model size, dataset size and problems. So Would appreciate if you could elaborate more on this part.

Weaknesses:
1. To be honest, from the evaluations, i really didn't see much differences compared to some of the other methods, one way to make this convincing is through inspecting the pathology regions, for example: https://github.com/microsoft/fastmri-plus - you can find a few examples with pathologies and see how your method performs. 

2. I don't think the methods you compared are very updated, try to compare with some diffusion-based models, starting from this paper: Robust Compressed Sensing MRI with Deep Generative Priors https://arxiv.org/abs/2108.01368 there are a bunch of works. e.g., Adaptive diffusion priors for accelerated MRI reconstruction.

3. One point you are selling is the efficiency, but i didn't see many efficiency analysis, ablations in the paper, and you really want control the # of parameters when doing the ablation studies.

QUESTIONS:
I have put my questions in the Strengths And Weaknesses section.

LIMITATIONS:
1. little performance improvements compared to other methods.
2. not comparing to updated literatures.
3. pathologies region reconstruction analysis.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
The paper introduces Time-Embedded Algorithm Unrolling (TE-Unroll) for deep learning–based MRI reconstruction, inspired by connections between vector AMP and diffusion models. The proposed method incorporates time-conditioned proximal networks (via FiLM embeddings), learnable time-varying scalars for data-fidelity weighting, and Onsager correction. It applies to multiple unrolling schemes (e.g., VSQP, ADMM) with ResNet or U-Net proximals. Extensive experiments on fastMRI knee and brain datasets at acceleration factors of 4/6/8 show consistent PSNR/SSIM improvements over shared and unshared baselines, with little parameter overhead. Ablation studies on unroll depth, optimizers, and qualitative results further validate the approach.

In the initial review, multiple concerns were raised by the reviewers, including questioning the utility and significance of the (improved) images reconstructed, the absence of comparison with the state-of-the-art baseline models, the lack of clarity around the notion of efficiency of the proposed method, and the poor motivation of the work. However, during the rebuttal period, the authors were able to sufficiently and convincingly address most of the reviewers' concerns, prompting the reviewers to update their scores. 

Keeping in mind the rebuttal posed by the authors and the subsequent changes promised in the final manuscript, this is a well-motivated, technically solid work backed up by extensive experiments and ablation studies on open-source datasets. Other strengths of the paper are: 
- Novel model that borrows the advantages from the time-embedding design in the diffusion model and the vector AMP algorithm
- Well-organized paper and easy to read
- Extensive experiments on the NYU fastMRI dataset are adequate, and evaluation is performed on different unrolling baselines
- The anecdotal human evaluation results gathered during the rebuttal period further strengthen the story
reviews.TITLE: Decision
